test_text.title: Inquiry about the West Bay Hotel's Temporary Staff Advertisement

audioscript.text: Section 1, you will hear a man telephoning to ask about a job in a hotel.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, West Bay Hotel.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, good morning.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'm ringing about your advertisement in the Evening Gazette.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Is that the one for temporary staff?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The advertisement is for temporary staff, so temporary has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, West Bay Hotel.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, good morning.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'm ringing about your advertisement in the Evening Gazette.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Is that the one for temporary staff?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, I'm afraid the person who's dealing with that isn't in today, but I can give you the main details if you like.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, please.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Could you tell me what kind of staff you're looking for?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: We are looking for waiters at the moment.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: There was one post for a cook, but that's already been taken.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Um. What are the hours of work?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: There are two different shifts.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: There is a day shift from 7 to 2, and a late shift from 4 to 11.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And can people choose which one they want to do?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Not normally, because everyone would choose the day shift, I suppose.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: You alternate from one week to another.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK, uh, I'm I'm just writing all this down.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: What about time off?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: You get one day off, and I think you can negotiate which one you want.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's more or less up to you.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: But it has to be the same one every week.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Do you know what the rates of pay are?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, I've got them here.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. You get £5.50 an hour.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And that includes a break.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Do I have to go home to eat or?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: You don't have to, you can get a meal in the hotel if you want to, and there's no charge for it, so you might as well.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, good.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, so let's see.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'd get er, two hundred and twenty one, no, no, two hundred and thirty one pounds a week.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: You'd also get tips.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Our guests tend to be quite generous.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Is there a uniform?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: What about clothes?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, I forgot to mention that.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: You need to wear a white shirt, just a plain one, and dark trousers, you know, not green or anything like that.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And we don't supply those.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's OK, I've got trousers.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I just have to buy a couple of shirts.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: What about anything else?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Do I need a waistcoat or anything?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: You have to wear a jacket, but the hotel lends you that.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I see.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh. One last thing.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I don't know what the starting date is.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Hmm. Just a minute.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I think it's some time around the end of June.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. Yes, the 28th in time for the summer.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's great, I'm available from the 10th.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, good.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, if you can call again, you need to speak to the service manager.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Her name's Jane Urwin, that's URWIN.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And she'll probably arrange to meet you.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK, and when's the best time to ring?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Could you call tomorrow?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Um. She usually starts checking the rooms at midday, so before then, if you can, so she'll have more time to chat.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'll just give you her number, because she's got a direct line.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Thanks.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, OK, uh, yes, I'll do that.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And by the way, she will ask you for a reference, so you might like to be thinking about that, you know, just someone who knows you and can vouch for you.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, no problem.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, thanks very much for your help.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: You're welcome.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Bye.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Bye.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Improvement Plan for Red Hill Suburb

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a man being interviewed on local radio about a plan to improve a suburb called Red Hill.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 13.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 11 to 13.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, and welcome again to Your City Today.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: With me today is Graham Campbell, a councillor from the city council.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: He'll be telling us about the plan to improve the fast growing suburb of Red Hill.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Good morning Graham and welcome to the show.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Good morning Carol.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Now Graham, I understand that there has been a lot of community consultation for the new plan.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Yes, we've tried to address some of the concerns that local groups told us about.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: People we've heard from are mainly worried about traffic in the area, and in particular the increasing speed of cars near schools.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: They feel that it's only a matter of time before there's an accident as a lot of children walk to the school.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: So, we're trying to do something about that.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Another area of concern is the overhead power lines.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: These are very old, and a lot of people we spoke to asked if something could be done about them.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Well, I'm happy to report that the power company have agreed to move the power lines underground at a cost of $800,000.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: I think that will really improve the look of the area, as well as being safer.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Hmm, that's good to know.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: But will that mean an increase in rates for the local businesses in that area?
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Well, the power company have agreed to bear the cost of this themselves after a lot of discussion with the council.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: This is wonderful news, as the council now has some extra funds for us to put into other things like tree planting and artwork.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the interview, you have some time to look at questions 14 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 14 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: We've also put together a map, which we sent out to all the residents in the area, and on the map we've marked the proposed changes.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Firstly, we'll plant mature pine trees to provide shelter and shade just to the right of the supermarket in Days Road.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: In order to address the traffic problems, the pavements on the corner of Carberry and Thomas Street will be widened.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: This will help to reduce the speed of vehicles entering Thomas Street.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: We think it's very important to separate the local residential streets from the main road, so the roadway at the entrance to Thomas Street from Days Road will be painted red.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: This should mark it more clearly, and act as a signal for traffic to slow down.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: One way of making sure that the pedestrians are safe is to increase signage at the intersections.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: A 'keep clear' sign will be erected at the junction of Evelyn Street and Hill Street, to enable traffic to exit at all times.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Something we're planning to do to help control the flow of traffic in the area is to install traffic lights halfway down Hill Street where it crosses Days Road.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Now, we haven't only thought about the cars and traffic of course, there's also something for the children.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: We're going to get school children in the area to research a local story, the life of a local sports hero perhaps, and an artist will incorporate that story into paintings on the wall of a building on the other side of Hill Street from the supermarket.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: And finally, we've agreed to build a new children's playground, which will be at the other end of Hill Street, close to the intersection with Carberry Street.
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: Wonderful.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Now, uh, what's the next stage?
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Well, the final plan will...
audioscript.speaker: GRAHAM

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Community groups are mainly concerned about
options: pedestrian safety.
traffic jams.
increased pollution.

items.title: It has been decided that the overhead power lines will be
options: extended.
buried.
repaired.

items.title: The expenses related to the power lines will be paid for by
options: the council.
the power company.
local businesses.

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear two students called Dan and Jeannie discussing their studies at university.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, Jeannie, how's it going?
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Oh, hello, Dan.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: Pretty well, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: Have you managed to get the money for the course yet?
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: Yes, that's all sorted out now, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: It took long enough though.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: It was practically a year ago that I applied to my local council for a grant, and it took them 6 months to turn me down.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: That's really slow.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: And I thought I was eligible for government funding, but it seems I was mistaken.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: So then I asked the boss of the company I used to work for if they would sponsor me, and much to my surprise, he said they'd make a contribution.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: But what about college grants and scholarships?
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: There must be some you could apply for.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: Yes, there are, but they're all so small that I decided to leave them until I was desperate.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Uh-huh.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: And in fact, I didn't need to apply.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: My parents had been saying that as I already had a job, I ought to support myself through college.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: But in the end, they took pity on me.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: So now I've just about got enough.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: That's good.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: So now I can put a bit of effort into meeting people.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: I Haven't had time so far.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Any suggestions?
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: What about joining some college clubs?
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: Oh right. You joined several didn't you?
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Yes, I'm in the drama club.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: It's our first performance next week, so we're rehearsing frantically.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: And I've got behind with my work.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: But it's worth it.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: I'm hoping to be in the spring production too.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: I've never liked acting.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Are you doing anything else?
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: I enjoyed singing when I was at school.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: So I joined a group when I came to college.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: I don't think the conductor stretches us enough though, so I'll give up after the next concert.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: And I also joined the debating society.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: It's fun, but with all the rehearsing I'm doing, something has to go, and I'm afraid that's the one.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: Do you do any sports?
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Yes, I'm in one of the hockey teams.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: I'm not very good, but I'd really miss it if I stopped.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: I decided to try tennis when I came to college, and I'm finding it pretty tough going.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: I'm simply not fit enough.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: Nor me.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: I think I'll give that a miss.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: I'm hoping it'll help me to build up my stamina, but it'll probably be a long haul.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: Good luck.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Thanks.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: How are you finding the course?
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: I wish we had more seminars.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: What?
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: I'd have thought we had more than enough already, all those people saying clever things that I could never think of.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: It's quite interesting, but I wonder if I'm clever enough to be doing this course.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: I find it helpful to listen to the other people.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: I like the way we're exploring the subject, and working towards getting insight into it.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: How do you get on with your tutor?
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: I don't think I'm on the same wavelength as mine, so I feel I'm not getting anything out of the tutorials.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: It would be more productive to read a book instead.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Oh, mine's very demanding.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: She gives me lots of feedback and advice, so I've got much better at writing essays.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: And she's helping me plan my revision for the end-of-year exams.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: Do tell me, I always struggle with revision.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Well, the first thing is to find out exactly what's required in the exams.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: Would it help to get hold of some past papers?
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: They'll help to make it clear.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: Right, I'll do that.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Then what?
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Then you can sort out your revision priorities based on what's most likely to come up.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: I put these on a card and read them through regularly.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: Uh-huh.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: But that isn't enough in itself.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: You also need a timetable to see how you can fit everything in the time available, then keep it in front of you while you're studying.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: I've done that before, but it hasn't helped me.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Maybe you need to do something different every day, so if you break down your revision into small tasks and allocate them to specific days, there's more incentive to tackle them.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: With big topics, you're more likely to put off starting.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: Good idea.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: And as I revise each topic, I write a single paragraph about it.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: Then later, I can read it through quickly.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: And it helps fix things in my mind.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: Well, that's brilliant.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: I also write answers to questions for the exam practice.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: It's hard to make myself do it though.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: Well, I'll try.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Thanks a lot, Jeannie.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: That's a great help.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: No problem.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: See you around.
audioscript.speaker: DAN

audioscript.text: Bye.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNIE

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: In which TWO ways is Dan financing his course?
options: He is receiving money from the government.
His family are willing to help him.
The college is giving him a small grant.
His local council is supporting him for a limited period.
A former employer is providing partial funding.

body.title: Which TWO reasons does Jeannie give for deciding to leave some college clubs?
options: She is not sufficiently challenged.
The activity interferes with her studies.
She does not have enough time.
The activity is too demanding physically.
She does not think she is any good at the activity.

items.title: What does Dan say about the seminars on the course?
options: The other students do not give him a chance to speak.
The seminars make him feel inferior to the other students.
The preparation for seminars takes too much time.

items.title: What does Jeannie say about the tutorials on the course?
options: They are an inefficient way of providing guidance.
They are more challenging than she had expected.
They are helping her to develop her study skills.

desc.constraint: NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS AND/OR A NUMBER

items: Make sure you know the exam requirements
Work out your <input> for revision and write them on a card
Make a <input> and keep it in view
Divide revision into <input> for each day
body.title: Advice on exam preparation

test_text.title: Australian Aboriginal Rock Art: Styles, Content, and the Rainbow Serpent

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a lecturer giving a talk about Australian rock art to a group of archaeology students.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning everyone.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I've been invited to talk about my research project into Australian Aboriginal rock paintings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The Australian Aborigines have recorded both real and symbolic images of their time on rock walls for many thousands of years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Throughout the long history of this tradition, new images have appeared, and new painting styles have developed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And these characteristics can be used to categorize the different artistic styles.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Among these are what we call the dynamic, yam, and modern styles of painting.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One of the most significant characteristics of the different styles is the way that humans are depicted in the paintings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The more recent paintings show people in static poses.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But the first human images to dominate rock art paintings over 8,000 years ago were full of movement.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These paintings showed people hunting and cooking food, and so they were given the name dynamic to reflect this energy.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's quite amazing considering they were painted in such a simple stick-like form. In the Yam period, there was a movement away from stick figures to a more naturalistic shape.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, they didn't go as far as the modern style, which is known as x-ray, because it actually makes a feature of the internal skeleton, as well as the organs of animals and humans.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The yam style of painting got its name from the fact that it featured much curvier figures that actually resembled the vegetable called yam, which is similar to a sweet potato.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The modern paintings are interesting because they include paintings at the time of the first contact with European settlers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Aborigines managed to convey the idea of the settlers' clothing by simply painting the Europeans without any hands,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: indicating the habit of standing with their hands in their pockets.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Size is another characteristic.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The more recent images tend to be life size or even larger.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But the dynamic figures are painted in miniature.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Aboriginal rock art also records the environmental changes that occurred over thousands of years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, we know from the dynamic paintings that over 8,000 years ago Aborigines would have rarely eaten fish, and sea levels were much lower at this time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In fact, fish didn't start to appear in paintings until the Yam period,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: along with shells and other marine images.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The paintings of the Yam tradition also suggest that during this time, the Aborigines moved away from animals as their main food source, and began including vegetables in their diet,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: as these feature prominently.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Fresh water creatures didn't appear in the paintings until the modern period from 4,000 years ago.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So these paintings have already taught us a lot, but one image that has always intrigued us is known as the Rainbow Serpent.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The Rainbow Serpent which is the focus of my most recent project, gets its name from its snake or serpent like body, and it first appeared in the Yam period 4 to 6,000 years ago.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Many believe it is a curious mixture of kangaroo, snake and crocodile, but we decided to study the rainbow serpent paintings to see if we could locate the animal that the very first painters based their image on.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The Yam period coincided with the end of the last ice Age.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This brought about tremendous change in the environment, with the sea levels rising and creeping steadily inland.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This flooded many familiar land features, and also caused a great deal of disruption to traditional patterns of life, hunting in particular.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: New shores were formed, and totally different creatures would have washed up onto the shores.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We studied 107 paintings of the rainbow serpent, and found that the one creature that matches it most closely was the ribboned pipefish,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: which is a type of seahorse.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This sea creature would have been a totally unfamiliar sight in the inland regions where the image is found, and it may have been the inspiration behind the early paintings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, at the end of the Ice Age, there would have been enormous changes in animal and plant life.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's not surprising then that the Aborigines linked this abundance to the new creatures they witnessed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Even today, Aborigines see the rainbow serpent as a symbol of creation, which is understandable given the increase in vegetation and the new life forms that featured when the image first appeared.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The Australian Aborigines have recorded both real and symbolic images of their time on rock walls for many thousands of years.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Throughout the long history of this tradition, new images have appeared, and new painting styles have developed.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And these characteristics can be used to categorize the different artistic styles.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Among these are what we call the dynamic, yam, and modern styles of painting.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One of the most significant characteristics of the different styles is the way that humans are depicted in the paintings.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The more recent paintings show people in static poses.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But the first human images to dominate rock art paintings over 8,000 years ago were full of movement.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These paintings showed people hunting and cooking food, and so they were given the name dynamic to reflect this energy.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's quite amazing considering they were painted in such a simple stick-like form. In the Yam period, there was a movement away from stick figures to a more naturalistic shape.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, they didn't go as far as the modern style, which is known as x-ray, because it actually makes a feature of the internal skeleton, as well as the organs of animals and humans.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The yam style of painting got its name from the fact that it featured much curvier figures that actually resembled the vegetable called yam, which is similar to a sweet potato.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The modern paintings are interesting because they include paintings at the time of the first contact with European settlers.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Aborigines managed to convey the idea of the settlers' clothing by simply painting the Europeans without any hands,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: indicating the habit of standing with their hands in their pockets.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Size is another characteristic.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The more recent images tend to be life size or even larger.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But the dynamic figures are painted in miniature.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Aboriginal rock art also records the environmental changes that occurred over thousands of years.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For example, we know from the dynamic paintings that over 8,000 years ago Aborigines would have rarely eaten fish, and sea levels were much lower at this time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In fact, fish didn't start to appear in paintings until the Yam period,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: along with shells and other marine images.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The paintings of the Yam tradition also suggest that during this time, the Aborigines moved away from animals as their main food source, and began including vegetables in their diet,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: as these feature prominently.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Fresh water creatures didn't appear in the paintings until the modern period from 4,000 years ago.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So these paintings have already taught us a lot, but one image that has always intrigued us is known as the Rainbow Serpent.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The Rainbow Serpent which is the focus of my most recent project, gets its name from its snake or serpent like body, and it first appeared in the Yam period 4 to 6,000 years ago.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Many believe it is a curious mixture of kangaroo, snake and crocodile, but we decided to study the rainbow serpent paintings to see if we could locate the animal that the very first painters based their image on.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The Yam period coincided with the end of the last ice Age.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This brought about tremendous change in the environment, with the sea levels rising and creeping steadily inland.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This flooded many familiar land features, and also caused a great deal of disruption to traditional patterns of life, hunting in particular.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: New shores were formed, and totally different creatures would have washed up onto the shores.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We studied 107 paintings of the rainbow serpent, and found that the one creature that matches it most closely was the ribboned pipefish,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: which is a type of seahorse.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This sea creature would have been a totally unfamiliar sight in the inland regions where the image is found, and it may have been the inspiration behind the early paintings.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, at the end of the Ice Age, there would have been enormous changes in animal and plant life.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's not surprising then that the Aborigines linked this abundance to the new creatures they witnessed.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Even today, Aborigines see the rainbow serpent as a symbol of creation, which is understandable given the increase in vegetation and the new life forms that featured when the image first appeared.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

text: Which painting styles have the following features?
textReadable: Which painting styles have the following features?

items: to identify the <input> used as the basis for the Rainbow Serpent
items.title: Aim of project:

items: unusual because it appeared in inland areas
items.title: Rainbow Serpent image

answer_key.correct_answer: sea level | water level | sea levels | water levels
